coredata.dc:title: MoS2 nanoflowers surface decorated with CuS nanorods and carbon dots for fluorescent and ultrasound imaging in cancer therapy

coredata.dc:description: In recent years, the design of various ultrasound responsive echogenic nanomaterials offers many advantages such as deep tissue penetration, high signal intensity, colloidal stability, biocompatibility and less expensive for ultrasound-based cancer cell imaging while providing the option to monitor the progress of tumor volume during the treatment. Further, the ability of nanomaterials to combine photo-thermal therapy (PTT) and chemotherapy has opened a new avenue in the development of cancer theranostics for synergistic cancer therapy. Herein, we report MoS2 nanoflowers (NFs) surface decorated with CuS nanorods (NRs) and folic acid-derived carbon dots (FACDs) using cystine-polyethyleneimine (PEI) linker for PTT-chemotherapy. The size of NFs was found to be 350 ± 50 nm which increased to 500 ± 50 nm after surface decoration. The morphology of MoS2 NFs before and after surface decoration was investigated using scanning electron microscopy, X-ray diffraction, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, and UV-Vis-NIR spectroscopy. The photo-thermal heat generation was found to be increasing as a function of the concentration of NFs. The encapsulation efficiency of doxorubicin (DOX) and photo-thermal conversion efficiency (PCE) for surface-decorated MoS2 NFs (MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs) was estimated to be 42 and 44 %, respectively. The surface decoration of CuS NRs and FACDs on MoS2 NFs not only improved the anticancer activity but also increased the signal intensity in ultrasound and fluorescence imaging of cancer cells. The MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs exhibited excellent cytotoxicity against MDA-MB-231 cancer cells. Hence, the hybrid system demonstrated here showed high potential for use as a combined probe for non-invasive ultrasound imaging and fluorescence imaging for PTT-chemotherapy.

SIR/2022/001264 and CRG/2020/004175) and the National Research Foundation (NRF), funded by the Ministry of Science & ICT (NRF-2021R1A2C3007817). The authors acknowledge the Department of Radiology, SRM Medical College and Hospital, SRM Institute of Science and Technology (SRMIST) for providing access to imaging facilities. We also thank SRM Central Instrumentation Facility, and Nanotechnology Research Centre (NRC), SRMIST for providing research facilities. The model specimen used in this research was commercially available edible dead fish, which does not require ethical approval.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HR-TEM investigation showing the morphology of MoS2 NFs (a, e), CuS NRs (b, f), FACDs (c, g) and MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs (d, h). Low magnification images (a-d) and high magnification images (e-h) of MoS2 NFs, CuS NRs, FACDs, and MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs.
a) XRD spectra of bare MoS2 NFs, CuS NRs, and MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs, and b) FTIR spectra of bare MoS2 NFs, CuS NRs, MoS2@CuS NFs and MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs.
UV- Vis spectra of bare MoS2 NFs, CuS NRs, FACDs, and MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs.
XPS investigation of (a) CuS NRs: (i) Cu2p and (ii) S2p; and (b) MoS2/CuS@FACDs NFs: (i) Mo3d, (ii) C1s, (iii) Cu2p and (iv) S2p.

Photo-thermal heat generation investigations of MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs as a function of time. (a) Heat generation at different concentrations, (b) single heating/cooling cycle, and (c) heating/cooling cycles up to 5 cycles for MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs at 100 µg/mL (808 nm, 0.5 W. cm−2).
The release of DOX-loaded MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs in the absence (a) and presence of NIR light (b) at 808 nm and 0.5 W. cm−2.

Fluorescence microscopy images of MDA-MB-231 (a), L-929 (b), and co-cultured cells (c) incubated with MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs, stained with AO and imaged at the excitation wavelength of 350 nm. Scale bar = 50 µm.

Fluorescence microscopy images of MDA-MB-231 and L-929 cells at high magnification in MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs treated co-cultured cells. The cells were incubated with MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs, stained with AO, and imaged at the excitation wavelength of 350 nm. Green channel (a), blue channel (b), and superimposed image (c). Cancer cells appear in bright blue in contrast to normal cells.

Cell viability of different nanomaterials against L-929 cells in the absence (a) and presence of NIR light of 808 nm (b). Cell viability of different nanomaterials against MDA-MB-231 cells in the absence (c) and presence of NIR light of 808 nm (d). The comparative experiment shows the anti-cancer activity of control cells, control cells+NIR, bare MoS2 NFs, MoS2 NFs+NIR, MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs, MoS2@CuS+NIR, MoS2@FACDs+NIR, MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs+NIR, free DOX, MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs+DOX and MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs+DOX+NIR (e). The obtained values are the mean of triplicates, and the significance of the sample was set at P < 0.05 when compared to the control. It was calculated by two-way ANOVA followed by Dennett’s multiple comparisons method. * ** *P < 0.0001 versus control and MoS2/CuS@FACDs, * **P < 0.001 versus MoS2/CuS@FACDs+DOX, * *P < 0.01 versus MoS2/CuS@FACDs+NIR and *P < 0.05 versus DOX.
The fluorescence microscopic investigation shows the anticancer activity of control cells, MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs, free DOX, MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs+DOX against MDA-MB-231 cells. Scale bar = 200 µm.
The fluorescence microscopic investigation shows the anticancer activity of control cells+NIR, MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs+NIR, and MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs+DOX+NIR against MDA-MB- 231 cells. Scale bar = 200 µm.

Photographic images show the balloons containing control (a), bare MoS2 NFs (b), CuS NRs (c), and MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs samples (d). The acquired ultrasound contrast images are provided for the same samples: control (a1), bare MoS2 NFs (b1), CuS NRs (c1), and MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs (d1). The images were captured in both harmonic mode and B-mode at 1 MHz. The photographic and contrast images for control fish (e) and fish injected with MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs (f), and the plot of contrast intensity profiles for different samples (g).
Schematic illustration showing the surface modification of MoS2 NFs with CuS NRs and FACDs to form MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In recent years, the design of various ultrasound responsive echogenic nanomaterials offers many advantages such as deep tissue penetration, high signal intensity, colloidal stability, biocompatibility and less expensive for ultrasound-based cancer cell imaging while providing the option to monitor the progress of tumor volume during the treatment. Further, the ability of nanomaterials to combine photo-thermal therapy (PTT) and chemotherapy has opened a new avenue in the development of cancer theranostics for synergistic cancer therapy. Herein, we report MoS2 nanoflowers (NFs) surface decorated with CuS nanorods (NRs) and folic acid-derived carbon dots (FACDs) using cystine-polyethyleneimine (PEI) linker for PTT-chemotherapy. The size of NFs was found to be 350 ± 50 nm which increased to 500 ± 50 nm after surface decoration. The morphology of MoS2 NFs before and after surface decoration was investigated using scanning electron microscopy, X-ray diffraction, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, and UV-Vis-NIR spectroscopy. The photo-thermal heat generation was found to be increasing as a function of the concentration of NFs. The encapsulation efficiency of doxorubicin (DOX) and photo-thermal conversion efficiency (PCE) for surface-decorated MoS2 NFs (MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs) was estimated to be 42 and 44 %, respectively. The surface decoration of CuS NRs and FACDs on MoS2 NFs not only improved the anticancer activity but also increased the signal intensity in ultrasound and fluorescence imaging of cancer cells. The MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs exhibited excellent cytotoxicity against MDA-MB-231 cancer cells. Hence, the hybrid system demonstrated here showed high potential for use as a combined probe for non-invasive ultrasound imaging and fluorescence imaging for PTT-chemotherapy.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The proposed MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs show the integration of ultrasound/fluorescence imaging and chemo-photothermal therapy.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, theranostic nanoagents have gained significant attention in the medical field for diagnosis and treatment of different diseases. Tumor therapies based on multifunctional nanomaterials are being rapidly developed for cancer treatment because of their ability to administer chemotherapeutic drugs more safely and effectively when compared to traditional chemotherapy, surgery, and radiotherapy [1,2]. Multi-model synergistic theranostics are preferred over mono-therapeutic systems due to their higher cytotoxicity, fewer side effects, and remotely controllable features for cancer treatment [3–5]. Though nanomaterials-based theranostic agents have shown high efficacy in pre-clinical studies, they face limitations such as easy identification and elimination from blood circulation by the reticuloendothelial system and undesired accumulation in non-targeted organs, thus hindering clinical translation by raising concerns about long-term side effects of accumulated nanomaterials in the body [3,6–9]. The design of ultrasound-responsive nano agents offers advantages such as non-invasive procedure, deep tissue penetration for medical imaging, less expensive, and option for ultrasound-trackable drug delivery vehicle [10,11]. Further, the ability of these nanomaterials to bypass biological barriers and specifically target cancer cells holds promise for revolutionizing cancer treatment [12,13] while allowing for vivid visualization of tumor changes and inflammation in targeted sites [6]. The use of various nanoparticles (NPs) such as carbon-based NPs [14], silica-based NPs [15], noble metal NPs [16,17], and magnetic NPs [18] along with the option of surface decoration with targeting ligands to specifically bind to cancer cells enables them to absorb light at specific wavelengths and enhances the therapeutic effect in PTT [19]. This dual-functionality in combination with their ability for encapsulation and release of therapeutic agents for personalized medicine allows tailoring of treatment strategies based on individual patient's responses.

Various carrier systems such as liposomes [20], polymer conjugates [21], micelles [22], perfluorocarbon nanodroplets (PFCnDs) [23], and microbubbles [24] have been demonstrated to generate harmonic signals and enhance signal intensity in ultrasound imaging. PFCnDs are often used as carrier systems for drug delivery because their resonance frequency falls between 1 and 12 MHz, which is commonly used in medical applications [23]. The polymer functionalization on the bubble surface not only provides stealth behavior in blood circulation and an option for targeted therapy but also enhances signal intensity in ultrasound field imaging [25]. Alternatively, semiconducting NPs such as quantum dots [26], copper sulfide (CuS) [27], molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) [28], ZnO2 [29], and CdTe NPs [30] are shown to improve the cytotoxicity against cancer cells by generating reactive oxygen species (ROS). While CuS NPs are synthesized through several methods, such as one-step solid-state reactions [31], hydrothermal processes [32], thermolytic reduction of copper thiolate precursors [33], and wet chemical methods [34], the folic acid derived carbon dots (FA-CDs) are predominantly synthesized via hydrothermal [35] and microwave-assisted methods [36]. Similarly, various methods such as hydrothermal [28], solvothermal [37], chemical vapor deposition [38], and template-assisted approaches [39] are used for the synthesis of different shaped MoS2 nanostructures. Recently, it has been demonstrated that the above-mentioned nanoparticle systems can improve the signal intensity when imaged under ultrasound imaging and photoacoustic imaging systems [40,41]. However, the poor colloidal stability and reduction in photothermal conversion efficiency (PCE) in biological fluids limits their potential in in-vivo clinical settings. Recently, two-dimensional (2D) ultrathin transition metal dichalcogenides (TMCDs) including MoS2 and tungsten disulfide (WS2) have been demonstrated as theranostic agents for synergetic cancer therapy due to their strong light absorption, high PCE and biocompatibility [28,42]. Especially for cancer therapy, MoS2 nanosheets are functionalized with targeting ligands and/or imaging probes to achieve specific molecular targeting and multimodal imaging capabilities [43]. Further, the surface functionalization also improves the colloidal stability and biocompatibility of MoS2-based nanothermal agents for in vitro applications. As MoS2-based nanomaterials show promise for ultrasound imaging, many attempts are being made to focus on their design, biocompatibility, and imaging performance for various biomedical applications [44]. For instance, by exploiting their high PCE and flexibility, Murugan et al., demonstrated their ability to mimic multiple enzymes in the environment of normal and tumor cells (e.g., nanozymes). In a separate study, MoS2 nanosheets modified with polyethylene glycol (PEG), quantum dots, and cyclic arginine–glycine–aspartic (RGD) showed enhanced fluorescent signal and high PCE when exposed to near infra-red (NIR) light of 785 nm [45].

In our previous investigation on the Drosophila melanogaster model, we have shown that decoration of nanoceria on the surface of MoS2 nanoflakes (NFs) using polyethyleneimine (PEI) linker (NCeO2-PEI-MoS2 NFs) has shown no significant effects on larval morphology, size, movement and flying ability demonstrating better biocompatibility for decorated NFs [46]. In the follow-up study, we have shown that the molecular weight of the PEI linker has a key role in controlling the PCE of resulting MoS2 NFs, thus significantly influencing the cytotoxicity against cancer cells [47]. Herein, we report MoS2 NFs surface decorated with folic acid (FA)-derived carbon quantum dots (CDs) and semiconducting CuS NRs for triple-negative breast cancer cell imaging and therapy. While the base structure of MoS2 NFs helps in photo-thermal heat generation, the decorated FACDs and CuS NRs play a key role in enhancing the signal intensity in fluorescence imaging and ultrasound imaging, respectively. Notably, Cu2 + ions can mimic the Fenton-like catalytic activity in cancer cells and superoxide dismutase and catalase-like activity in normal cells [28]. As a result, the proposed nanozymes can generate heat by absorbing NIR light and specifically target the triple-negative breast cancer cells while allowing us to track the progress of the treatment procedure through fluorescent imaging and a medical-range ultrasound scanning system. Additionally, we encapsulate the anti-cancer drug, doxorubicin hydrochloride (DOX) in the nanozymes and demonstrate their potential in improving anticancer activity synergistically for chemotherapy-PTT. Hence, the ability to combine imaging and therapeutic capabilities in a single agent might open up new possibilities for personalized medicine and targeted cancer theranostics.

Copper nitrate trihydrate (Cu(NO3)2.3H2O), sodium citrate (Na3C6H5O7), thioacetamide (C2H5NS), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), hydrochloric acid (HCl), N-hydroxy succinimide (NHS), N-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-N-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) and phosphate buffered saline (PBS) were purchased from Sisco Research Laboratory, India. Ammonium molybdate tetrahydrate ((NH4)6Mo7O24.4H2O), thiourea (CH4N2S), folic acid (FA), L-cysteine, branched polyethyleneimine (PEI, Mw= 25,000 kDa) were all purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, India. Fetal bovine serum (FBS), trypsin, ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), alamarBlue™, penicillin, and streptomycin, 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT), and dulbecco’s modified eagle medium (DMEM) were all purchased from Thermofisher, India. Acridine orange (AO) and propidium iodide (PI) were purchased from HIMedia Laboratories, India. Milli-Q water with a resistivity of ≥ 18 MΩ·cm was used for all the experiments (Merck- Millipore water purification system, France).

The MoS2 NFs were synthesized using the hydrothermal method, as reported previously [28]. In brief, 50 mg of ammonium molybdate and 50 mg of thiourea were dissolved in 75 mL of milli-Q water under continuous stirring for 30 min. After adjusting the pH to 3, the solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated to 180 °C for 20 h in a hot air oven (Jothi Enterprises Pvt. Ltd., India). After cooling naturally to room temperature, the black precipitate obtained was washed three times with milli-Q water and ethanol through a centrifugation process at 8000 rpm for 10 min to eliminate impurities and unreacted precursors. The final MoS2 NFs were dried overnight in a hot air oven at 60 °C and stored for further use.

The CuS NRs were prepared using a hydrothermal method as reported in our previous publication [27]. In brief, 50 mg of copper nitrate trihydrate was dissolved in 50 mL of milli-Q water under continuous stirring. Then, 100 mg of sodium citrate and 100 mg of thioacetamide were added to the copper nitrate solution and stirring was continued for 1 h. After the formation of a green-colored precipitate, the dispersion was transferred to a stainless-steel Teflon-lined autoclave and heated to 180 °C for 6 h. After cooling the autoclave to room temperature, the formed CuS NRs were washed multiple times with water and ethanol to remove the impurities and other contaminants. The final CuS NRs were dried overnight in a hot air oven at 60 °C and stored for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of folic acid-derived carbon dots (FACDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The FACDs were prepared by carbonization of FA at 200 °C using hydrothermal method [35]. In brief, 50 mg of FA was dissolved in 45 mL of milli-Q water under continuous stirring for 30 min. The solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, and heated to 200 °C for 12 h. After the reaction, the autoclave was cooled down naturally to room temperature. The suspension containing FACDs was added to a dialysis bag, washed several times with water, and stored at 4 °C for future use.

The decoration of CuS NRs on the surface of MoS2 NFs was performed in two consecutive steps. The surface modification of MoS2 NFs with L- cystine (Cys) via disulfide bonds followed by sequential attachment of PEI through EDC/NHS covalent linkage resulted in free dangling Cys-PEI linker molecules on the surface of MoS2 NFs. Later, the PEI branches with -NH2 groups were effectively used to decorate CuS NRs and FACDs by mixing them with NP suspensions in a sequential manner. In brief, the aqueous suspension (25 mL) containing 0.2 g of MoS2 NFs was mixed with 1 mL of Cys solution (25 mg) to decorate Cys molecules on the MoS2 surface via disulfide bonds. Later, 0.1 M EDC (1 mL) and 0.1 M NHS (1 mL) solutions were added in a drop-wise manner under continuous stirring for 4 h at 37 °C. After adjusting the pH to 6.8 using 0.1 M of HCl solution and washing multiple times with water, the suspension was mixed with CuS NRs (1 mL, 0.25 mg/mL) under continuous stirring at room temperature for 1 h. The suspension was washed multiple times with water to remove the free CuS NRs present in the supernatant. To attach FACDs on the surface of MoS2 NFs, the suspension was again mixed with aqueous FACDs suspension and the stirring was continued up to 4 h, as shown in Scheme 1. The obtained MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs were washed many times with water, dried under vacuum at 60 °C, and stored for further studies.

The encapsulation of DOX was performed by dispersing MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs in 1 mL of drug solution (5 mg/mL) for 2 h at room temperature. After completion of the drug encapsulation process, the NFs were collected by centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 10 min, and the absorbance of the supernatant was measured at 485 nm using UV-Vis spectroscopy. The amount of encapsulation and encapsulation efficiency (EE) was determined by measuring the absorbance difference in the supernatant before and after the encapsulation process. The DOX-loaded NFs were washed two times with water to remove the free drug present in the supernatant. Further, the EE was calculated using the following equation [48]:
EE% = (Total amount of drug loaded in the sample/ Initial amount of drug)* 100

The DOX-loaded MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs were dispersed in acetate buffer (pH 5.5) and PBS (pH 7.4) to simulate the tumor environment and plasma pH and the release was studied as a function of time. For release experiments, a series of samples was prepared from a single batch in 2 mL centrifuge tubes and incubated at 37 °C and 150 rpm in an incubator shaker. At a particular time interval, one of the samples was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 5 min, and drug release was estimated by directly measuring the supernatant absorbance at 485 nm via UV-Vis spectroscopy. The other samples were replaced with fresh acetate buffer and PBS to maintain the concentration gradient between the supernatant and MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs. Further, the release was also investigated under NIR light exposure to study light-induced release.

The photo-thermal heat generation of synthesized MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs was evaluated by exposing the suspension to NIR light in the concentration range between 0 and 100 µg/mL. The heat generation was observed as a function of time by irradiating the NF suspension using NIR laser light of 808 nm at 0.5 W·cm−2 (Solid State Laser Set-Up, Hitsan, India). The rise in temperature of the suspension was measured using a thermocouple. The photo-stability of the fabricated NFs was examined by exposing the NF suspension with NIR light for 5 min (heating cycle) followed by cooling of the suspension for 5 min (laser OFF condition). The consecutive heating and cooling cycles were analyzed up to five cycles to investigate the photo-stability of fabricated MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs. The PCE was estimated using the heat dissipation constant obtained from heating/cooling cycles as reported in the literature [27,28]. For control experiments, only milli-Q water was exposed to NIR laser light of 808 nm at 0.5 W·cm−2 for 5 min. The PCE (η) was calculated using the following formula:
η= hs(Tmax-Tsurr)-Q0/ I (1-10-A)
where η is the photothermal conversion efficiency (%), h is the heat transfer coefficient (W.m−2K−1); S is the surface area (m²), Tmax and Tsur are the maximum and initial temperatures (K), Q0 is the heat dissipation to the atmosphere (W); I is the laser power (W); and A is the absorbance of MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs at 808 nm.

The normal fibroblast cells (L-929) and triple-negative breast cancer cells (MDA-MB-231) were obtained from the National Centre for Cell Sciences (NCCS), Pune, India. The cells were maintained in a DMEM medium with 10 % FBS, 1 % streptomycin, and 1 % penicillin in a humidified incubator with 5 % CO2 at 37 °C. At 80–85 % confluence, the cells were trypsinized in PBS with 0.52 mM EDTA and 0.25 % trypsin and retrieved for further study. This level of confluence indicates that a significant portion of the surface area is covered by a dense layer of cells, suggesting healthy cell growth and adequate proliferation under the given conditions.

The cell viability of L-929 and MDA-MB-231 cells was evaluated by incubating the cells in the presence of different nanomaterials such as MoS2 NFs, CuS NRs, and nanozymes for 24 h via Alamar blue assay. In brief, the cells were seeded in a 96-well plate (4 ×104 cells/well) (Tarsons, India) and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. The cells were treated with different concentrations of nanomaterials (0–100 µg/mL) and then incubated for 20 h. After multiple washing with PBS, 10 µL of alamar blue solution (1 mg/mL) was added to the cell suspension and incubated for 4 h. Finally, the cells were washed with DMSO and the absorbance was measured at 570 and 600 nm using a microplate reader. To evaluate the normal and cancer cell apoptosis induced by various nanomaterials, the experiments were also repeated at different concentrations and in the presence and absence of NIR light exposure.

For anti-cancer activity studies, the MDA-MB-231 cells were cultured in a 6-well plate (Tarsons, India) at a cell density of 1 × 105 cells/well for 20 h at 37 °C. Subsequently, the cells were treated with 100 μg/mL of nanomaterials including MoS2 NFs, CuS NRs, and MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs for 24 h. After the treatment, the cells were washed with PBS and added with 10 µL of AO (1 mg/mL) and PI (1 mg/mL) for 5 min at 37 °C. The presence of live and dead cell populations was investigated by fluorescence microscopy. Separately, a set of experiments in the presence and absence of NIR light for 5 min at 808 nm and 0.5 W·cm−2 was also performed to evaluate the role of NIR light in enhancing the anticancer activity.

The ability of MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs as a contrast agent for US imaging was performed using a clinical ultrasound scanning system available at SRM Medical College and Research Centre, Kattankulathur, Tamilnadu, India (LOGIQ P5 transducer (L1–3), LOGIQTM systems, GE Healthcare, U.S.A.). To acquire ultrasound imaging and reduce background noise from the sample, a phantom was prepared by placing a sample-filled finger-shaped small ballon in a large fluid-filled (50 mL of milli-Q water) ultrasound transparent balloon. The balloon filled with only water was used for control experiments. The imaging was performed by touching the surface of the balloon with the tip of the transducer in B-mode. To investigate the MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs for ex-vivo imaging, commercially available dead fish (red snapper, Sankara) was bought from the fish market, washed thoroughly with water, injected with MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs suspension in a specific location, and imaged under the ultrasound imaging system at 1 MHz. The use of commercially available edible dead fish as the model specimen in this research eliminates the need for ethical approval, while still providing valuable insights that can be comparable with complex animal models.

For co-culture experiments, the MDA-MB-231 cells (1 ×105 cells/well) were seeded in 6-well plates containing 22 mm of cover slips and incubated for 20 h at 37 °C in a 5 % CO2 atmosphere. Later, the L-929 cells were seeded in the wells and incubated for 12 h. After the addition of MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs (100 µg), the suspension was incubated for 6 h, washed with PBS, and imaged under a fluorescence microscope at the excitation wavelength of 350 nm (F-7000, Fluorescence Microscope, Japan).

The composition and morphological characterization of MoS2 NFs, CuS NRs, FACDs, and MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs was investigated by field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, AperoS, ThermoFisher Scientific, Singapore), and high-resolution transmission electron microscope operating at 200 kV (HR-TEM, 2100 plus, JEOL, Japan). The optical absorption characteristics of MoS2 NFs, CuS NRs, FACDs, and MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs were examined by UV-Vis spectroscopy (Nanodrop 2000c spectrophotometer, USA). UV-Vis-NIR spectra of all samples were measured at a concentration of 100 µg/mL to maintain the signal intensity constant to a possible extent. The spectra were obtained in the wavelength range between 200 and 1100 nm (Cary 5000, Agilent Technologies, USA). The emission spectra for all the samples were acquired using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (FLS 1000, Edinburgh Instruments, U.K.). The functional group analysis using Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy for different samples such as MoS2 NFs, CuS NRs, FACDs, and MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs was performed in the range between 400 and 4000 cm−1 in transmission mode (Nicolet 6700 FTIR, ThermoFisher Scientific, USA). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) experiments were performed at an angle (2θ) varying from 10 to 80° (Benchtop, PANalytical, Netherlands). The chemical composition and valence states of different elements for the samples were investigated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (PHI VERSAPROBE III, Spectra Research Corporation, Canada). The surface charge and colloidal stability of MoS2 NFs, CuS NRs, FACDs, and MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs were investigated by dynamic light scattering technique (SZ-100 Analyzer, Horiba Scientific, Japan).

The synthesis of MoS2 NFs using ammonium molybdate and thiourea as precursors was performed via hydrothermal method as reported in our previous study [28]. Subsequently, the surface of NFs was decorated sequentially using Cys and PEI via disulfide bonding and covalent cross-linking by carbodiimide chemistry, respectively. After surface modification of MoS2 NFs with Cys—PEI linker, the surface charge reversal was observed from – 15.7 ± 5 mV to + 17 ± 5 mV indicating the presence of Cys—PEI linkers, most likely in the form of freely floating dangling molecules (Figure S1, Supporting Information). To develop nanozymes, MoS2 NFs were decorated with FACDs and CuS NRs in separate steps to explore their potential for targeting the triple-negative cancer cells while exploiting them for both fluorescent and ultrasound imaging. HR-TEM investigation has revealed the formation of flower-shaped MoS2 NFs with a size of 350 ± 50 nm (
Fig. 1 a). Notably, the average sizes of synthesized CuS NRs and FACDs were estimated to be 50 ± 5 nm and 10 ± 4 nm, respectively (Fig. 1b and c, Figure S2, Supporting Information). The dynamic light scattering experiments on CuS NRs and FACDs showed the surface potential of −13.7 and −17.3 mV, respectively (Figure S1, Supporting Information), thus offering an option to decorate the dangling Cys—PEI linker molecules with CuS NRs and FACDs via electrostatic interactions. After surface decoration, the surface potential of MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs was observed to be reversing from + 17 ± 5 mV to −20.4 mV indicating the successful attachment of CuS NRs and FACDs to the dangling Cys—PEI linker molecules (Figure S1, Supporting Information). To study the decoration of CuS NRs and FACDs on the surface of bare MoS2 NFs, the morphological change of MoS2 NFs was also investigated by field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) and HR-TEM investigations as shown in Figure S3, Supporting Information, and Fig. 1d and h. Notably, the uniform distribution of rod and spherical-shaped NPs on the surface of MoS2 NFs demonstrated the successful decoration of CuS NRs and FACDs. The estimated d-spacing values were found to be 0.22 nm and 0.31 nm showing the presence of (105) and (002) planes arising from CuS NRs and FACDs, respectively (Fig. 1 f-g). The presence of Cu, S, and Mo was also identified by energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) investigations of MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs confirming the successful decoration of CuS NRs and FACDs on the surface of MoS2 NFs (Figure S4, Supporting Information). To gain more insight into the colloidal stability of formed MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs, they were dispersed separately in milli-Q water and DMEM medium and investigated using dynamic light scattering technique. Even after 2 h of incubation, the MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs were found to be stable in both milli-Q water and DMEM medium as polydispersity index was found to be < 0.3 (Figure S5 , Supporting Information).

The crystalline structure of MoS2 NFs, CuS NRs, and MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs was also investigated by XRD as shown in Fig. 2 a. The XRD spectra of MoS2 NFs, CuS NRs, and MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs were matched with the crystalline peaks of JCPDS card of MoS2 (037–1492) and CuS (01–078–0877), respectively, to gain more detailed information about the presence of different crystalline planes. Notably, all the major characteristic peaks were observed in the respective spectra. For instance, MoS2 NFs revealed two diffraction peaks corresponding to (101) and (110) planes at 2θ values of 35° and 58°, respectively. Similarly, the CuS NRs showed sharper crystalline peaks at 27.7°, 29.5°, 32°, 32.8°, 48.2°, 52.8°, 59°, and 73.9° which are corresponding to (100), (102), (103), (006), (112), (108), (116) and (208) planes, respectively. Though MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs showed characteristic peaks of both CuS NRs and MoS2 NFs, as expected, the peak broadening and lowering of crystallinity was observed owing to sequential surface modification processes. Further, the weak diffraction peaks of MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs observed at 2θ values of 42° and 57° could be assigned to (006) plane of MoS2 NFs and (202) planes of CuS NRs, respectively [49,50]. The peak intensities are believed to be increased after surface functionalization due to the influence of PEI on the surface of MoS2 NFs. The presence of these peaks is also confirming the successful formation of MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs.

Furthermore, the MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs were also investigated by FTIR spectroscopy to understand more information about their surface functional groups and interaction between them. While bare MoS2 NFs showed the characteristic peaks of ν(Mo-O) δ(Mo-O) and S-S vibration bands at 919, 754, and 653 cm−1, respectively, the CuS NRs revealed strong metal-sulfur (Cu-S) stretching vibration bands at 612 cm−1. Two bands that appeared at 1025 and 1112 cm−1 could be assigned to –COO– stretching vibrations of adsorbed trisodium citrate on the surface of CuS NRs, as shown in Fig. 2 b. Further, the characteristic peaks observed at 3431, 2930, 1621, 1530, 1374, and 855 cm−1 correspond to –OH stretching vibrations arising from absorbed water molecules, -CH2 symmetric vibrations and asymmetric vibrations, stretching vibration of amide-I band present in PEI, C-H bending vibrations [51] and C-S stretching vibrations coming out of sulfur-containing compounds, most likely from MoS2-Cys. Though new amide bond formation between Cys and PEI after EDC-NHS cross-linking is not observed due to overlapping with amide-I bands arising from thiourea at 1621 cm−1, the disappearance of amide-II bands comes from N—H bending at 1530 cm−1 and formation of a broader peak at 1621 cm−1 for MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs confirms new amide bond formation between Cys and PEI. It is worth noting that even before the surface decoration of FACDs, MoS2@CuS NFs have characteristic bands of carboxyl groups at 1621 cm−1 (CO stretching from –COOH) and C–O–C stretching bands at 1374 and 1112 cm−1 arising from the reducing agent, citric acid (trisodium citrate). Hence, the spectrum appears the same before and after surface functionalization with FACDs. However, the increase in the intensity of above mentioned peaks confirms the successful decoration of FACDs on the surface of MoS2@CuS NFs as more number of carboxyl groups are contributed by FACDs. Further, the intensity of NH bands arising from PEI at 855 cm−1 also increased after FACDs decoration. This also confirms FACDs decoration on the surface of MoS2/CuS NFs as there was the addition of NH bands from FACDs.

The optical absorption properties of bare MoS2 NFs, CuS NRs, FACDs, and MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs were investigated by UV-Vis spectroscopy, as shown in Fig. 3 . Bare MoS2 NFs showed the characteristic peak at 218 nm which corresponds to the conjugation of thiol groups, indicating n-σ* transition [47,52]. A broad peak associated with the d-d transition of Cu2+ ions was observed at 403 nm for CuS NRs [27,53]. Notably, FACDs showed two characteristic bands at 233, 285, and 315 nm, which are associated with π–π* transition and n- π* of the pteridine ring and para-aminobenzoic acid, respectively [54]. The appearance of all these peaks in MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs spectrum confirms the successful decoration of CuS NRs and FACDs on the surface of MoS2 NFs. The fluorescence emission characteristics of bare MoS2 NFs, FACDs, CuS NRs, and MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs were also analyzed by photoluminescence spectroscopy (Figure S6, Supporting information). While a characteristic emission peak was observed for FACDs in the blue region at 410 nm, the multi-layered MoS2 NFs showed a sharp peak at 462 nm. Notably, MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs revealed two emission peaks, a broad peak belonged to FACDs at 395 nm and a sharp peak arising from MoS2 NFs at 482 nm. The overlapping of CuS characteristic peak at 395 nm and FACDs broad peak at 410 nm is believed to be the reason for the blue shifting of broad FACDs peak in MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs. Though the peak belonged to MoS2 NFs found to be red-shifted in MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs, the presence of characteristic peaks of FACDs, MoS2 NFs, and CuS NRs confirmed the successful decoration of FACDs and CuS NRs.

The chemical nature of MoS2/CuS@FACDs NFs was also investigated using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, as shown in Fig. 4 . The survey scan of CuS and MoS2/CuS@FACDs NFs are provided in (Figure S7, Supporting Information). The characteristic peaks of MoS2 NFs were observed at 226.8, 231.25, 233.71, and 236.03 eV, which are corresponding to S2s, Mo 3d5/2, Mo 3d3/2 and Mo4+, respectively (Fig. 4b (i)). The S2p spectrum showed the oxidation state of sulphur (S-S) characteristic peaks associated with 2p1/2 at 160.80, 161.59, and 162.60 eV, and the doublet peaks corresponding to 2p3/2 at 168.33 and 169.25 eV, respectively [55]. Notably, the C1s spectrum of MoS2/CuS@FACDs NFs revealed the characteristic peaks of C-C, C-O, and CO at the binding energy of 284.60, 285.9, and 288.5, respectively (Fig. 4b (ii)). The Cu2p spectrum showed the first doublet peak of Cu2p3/2 and Cu2p1/2 at 933.02 and 941.09 eV indicating the Cu+ oxidation state. The second doublet peak of Cu2p1/2 and satellite peak observed at the binding energies of 960.72 and 973.51 eV revealed the Cu2+ oxidation state of metal-sulfide bonds [55], as shown in Fig. 4b (iii).

The photo-thermal heat generation of MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs was investigated using NIR light of 808 nm at 0.5 W.cm−2, as shown in Fig. 5 . The heat generation was investigated as a function of time by varying the concentration of MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs from 10 to 100 µg/mL. The suspension temperature was increased from 29.1 to 41.3 °C at 10 µg/mL of nanomaterials which increased to 57.9 °C when the concentration was changed from 10 to 100 µg/mL. Notably, the PCE was found to be 44.2 % for MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs at a concentration of 100 µg/mL. To gain more detailed information about the photo-stability of surface decorated NFs, the heating and cooling cycles were continued up to 5 cycles. For one cycle, the MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs were exposed to NIR light at 808 nm for 5 min followed by cooling the suspension for 5 min by switching off the laser light. The temperature increase or decrease was measured as a function of time. Surprisingly, the temperature increase was not influenced by the number of cycles demonstrating good photo-stability of the decorated MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs. Notably, the PCE reported for the proposed MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs was found to be comparatively higher when compared to MoS2 NFs decorated with other polymeric systems. For instance, MoS2 NFs decorated with PEG showed PCE of 27.6 % at a concentration of 80 µg/mL under NIR irradiation of 2 W/cm² [7]. Murugan et al. reported that MoS2-PEI-NCeO2 NFs achieved a PCE of 27.7 % with a temperature rise of up to 53.7 °C at a concentration of 100 µg/mL under exposure to NIR light of 808 nm and power density of 0.5 W/cm² [28,47]. In comparison, the MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs demonstrated here showed PCE of 44.2 %, with a temperature increase of up to 57.9 °C at a concentration of 100 µg/mL and a power density of 0.5 W/cm² (Figure S8, Supporting Information). It is worth noting that the heat generation is contributed by both MoS2 and CuS NRs. Our previous investigations confirm that even bare MoS2 NFs or CuS NRs demonstrate high photo-thermal heat generation at the same conditions [27,28]. Additionally, we have also included a video to demonstrate the photo-thermal heat generation as a function of time, as shown in Video File VS1, Supporting Information. For measurements, the sample suspension was connected with a thermocouple, and exposed to 808 nm laser light to record the temperature increase over a period of 5 min. To minimize the video time, the video speed was increased and compressed to 30 s (originally from 5 min). Thus, we believe that the photo-thermal heat-generating abilities of both MoS2 NFs and CuS NRs might have contributed synergistically to improve the PCE of MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs.

The DOX encapsulation of MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs was found to be ∼42 %, which is equivalent to 0.4 mg/mg of NFs. The release experiments were performed in acetate buffer and PBS at pH 5.5 and 7.4 to simulate tumor and plasma conditions, respectively. At both conditions, the release was found to be increased as a function of time up to 6 h and saturated after that. The release percentages were estimated to be 29.3 and 6.7 % at pH 5.5 and 7.4 after 6 h, respectively ( Fig. 6). Separately a new set of experiments was also performed in the presence of NIR light of 808 nm at 0.5 W.cm−2 to study the role of NIR in improving the DOX release. The release percentages were increased to 46 and 36 % at pH 5.5 and 7.4, respectively, under NIR light exposure. Notably, the difference in release between pH 5.5 and 7.4 was estimated to be 22 % in the absence of NIR light. However, it was reduced to 10 % in the presence of NIR light. Often, it is expected that the encapsulation is most likely governed by both electrostatic interactions and physical entrapment. While electrostatic interactions can be controlled by changing pH, the physical entrapment can be significantly influenced by temperature increase as elevated temperature can enhance the release process. As a result, the release was increased at both pH conditions in the presence of NIR light. As expected, photo-thermal heat generation and pH play a key role in improving the DOX release for MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs.

The ability of MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs for cancer cell targeting and fluorescence imaging was investigated by adding surface decorated NFs in L-929, MDA-MB-231, and co-culture model suspensions followed by fluorescence imaging at 350 nm. For co-culture experiments, both cell lines were co-cultured at equivalent concentrations and allowed to reach the confluence. After incubating the cells with MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs for 6 h, they were subsequently stained with AO for live cell imaging. While the MDA-MB-231 cells appeared in blue after treatment with surface decorated NFs, there was hardly any fluorescence in suspension containing L-929 cells demonstrating the ability of MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs in identifying and targeting only MDA-MB-231 cells, as shown in Fig. 7 a-c, and Figure S9 , Supporting Information . To gain a better understanding of the targeting ability of surface decorated NFs, co-cultured cells containing both L-929 and MDA-MB-231 were added with MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs and imaged under a fluorescence microscope. Surprisingly, a bright blue fluorescence was observed in a subset of the cells indicated by white arrows, likely corresponding to the MDA-MB-231cells (Fig. 7 c). Cells exhibiting little to no blue fluorescence indicated by red arrows in Fig. 7 c, are presumably L-929 cells. Thus, only cancer cells appeared in bright blue when compared to normal cells confirming high affinity for cancer cells ( Figs. 7 c and 8). The presence of FA residues on FACDs of MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs allows them to selectively interact with folate receptors present on cancer cell membranes. However, the absence of folate receptors on normal cells limits their attachment with NFs, thus preventing them from appearing in blue color. Our previous investigation demonstrates that the disulfide bonds connecting cysteine to the MoS2 surface get ruptured in the presence of glutathione (GSH) after 20 min of exposure [47,56]. It is understood from the literature that the intracellular matrix of cancer cells has about 10 mM of GSH. Once the disulfide bonds are ruptured, there is a high possibility that polymeric linker along with surface decorated FACDs can enter into the cells via the FR-mediated endocytosis process, thus resulting in blue fluorescence inside the cells. Hence, this design demonstrates the potential of MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs for targeted cancer therapy and fluorescence imaging.

The biocompatibility of synthesized bare MoS2 NFs, CuS NRs, and MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs was performed against L-929 cells via alamar blue assay, as shown in Fig. 9 . The cell viability of various nanomaterials was evaluated at different concentrations ranging from 0 to 200 μg/mL at 37 °C for 24 h in the presence and absence of NIR laser light (808 nm and 0.5 W.cm−2). Though cell viability decreased as a function of concentration, the MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs found to be biocompatible up to 200 μg/mL. At 100 μg/mL, the cell viability of MoS2 NFs against L-929 cells was estimated to be 79.4 and 80.7 % in the absence and presence of NIR light, respectively ( Fig. 9 a and b). However, it was found to be 84.6 and 81.7 % for MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs in the absence and presence of NIR light, respectively, indicating significant improvement in biocompatibility when compared to bare MoS2 NFs. In other words, the surface decoration of CuS NRs and FACDs on MoS2 NFs enhanced the cell viability of L-929 cells. The same observation was also confirmed in a fluorescent microscopic investigation involving L-929 cells and different samples such as bare MoS2 NFs, CuS NRs, and MoS2/CuS@FACDs NFs (Figure S10, Supporting Information). Separately, a new set of experiments was also performed against MDA-MB-231 cells to investigate the anti-cancer activity of MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs. Surprisingly, the cytotoxicity of MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs was found to be increased as a function of concentration when compared to bare MoS2 NFs (Fig. 9 c and d). At 100 μg/mL, the cell viability percentages were estimated to be ∼ 57.6, 54.7, and 53.8 % for bare MoS2 NFs, CuS NRs and MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs, respectively. On the other hand, the cell viability percentage was found to be ∼ 37.3, 30.2, and 18 % for bare MoS2 NFs, CuS NRs, and MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs at the concentration of 100 μg/mL under NIR light exposure, respectively. Further, the cell viability percentages of MoS2@CuS NRs+NIR and MoS2@FACDs NFs+NIR were estimated to be ∼54.3, and 69.2 %, respectively (Figure S11, Supporting Information). Control experiments performed with control cells and control cells with NIR light exposure showed no cytotoxicity against MDA-MB-231 cells.

The ability to encapsulate and release DOX in response to pH changes endows MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs with high potential for chemo-PTT. Since they are envisaged to be a potential candidate for bimodal therapy, it becomes important to investigate their potential for chemo-PTT. Initially, the IC50 value of DOX against the MDA-MB-231 cell line was determined to be ∼2.815 µM (Figure S12, Supporting Information). To analyze the morphological alterations and cytotoxicity in cancer cells, they were treated with different samples as mentioned above and subsequently stained with AO and PI. AO easily permeates into live and apoptotic cells resulting in green fluorescence for both cell types by binding with DNA and RNA. In contrast, PI exclusively labels apoptotic cells with compromised membranes and produces red fluorescence. In control samples, most of the cells exhibited green fluorescence demonstrating the healthy live cell population. Anticancer efficacy was evaluated by treating MDA-MB-231 cells with bare MoS2 NFs, MoS2@CuS/FACDs, MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs + NIR, free DOX, MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs + DOX, and MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs + DOX + NIR. Treatment with free DOX, MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs + DOX, and MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs + DOX + NIR resulted in cell viabilities of 32.4, 26.4 and 11.3 %, respectively (
Figs. 10 and 11). The fluorescence microscopy experiments involving DOX should be carefully designed as DOX can emit red fluorescence that leads to false results of cell death. As a result, it should be compared with the MTT assay wherein DOX does not interfere in the analysis. On the other hand, in the absence of DOX, the emission of red fluorescence in the other samples demonstrates cell death. In summary, treatment with MoS2/CuS@FACDs + DOX under exposure to NIR light of 808 nm significantly enhanced cell killing, indicating a marked improvement in therapeutic efficacy.

The echogenicity and imaging capabilities of MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs were evaluated using an ultrasound medical imaging system with a 1 MHz L1–3 transducer on a LOGIQ-5 scanner, in both B-mode and harmonic mode. While the ultrasound contrast images of control and MoS2 NFs samples exhibited weak signal intensity, the suspension containing CuS NRs exhibited a greater number of bright spots indicating the improvement in the signal intensity ( Fig. 12). Notably, the MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs exhibited a much higher signal intensity than free CuS NRs. The signal intensity of MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs was estimated to be 200 % and 50 % higher than MoS2 NFs and CuS NRs, respectively (Fig. 12 g). The enhanced ultrasound signal intensity of MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs is attributed to the high scattering ability of CuS NRs and MoS2 NFs [52,53]. To demonstrate their potential in real-time clinical settings, ex-vivo imaging was performed using a commercially purchased fish to assess the contrast enhancement capabilities of MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs in a medical ultrasound imaging system operating at 1 MHz, as depicted in Fig. 12 e and f. Notably, there were significant differences in signal contrast between the images acquired before and after the administration of MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs. To our delight, the image acquired after administration of MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs showed minute features clearly when compared to the control image. Further, a significant contrast enhancement was observed in a larger area indicating the extent of spreading of the NF suspension. It is believed that the acoustic impedance mismatch between MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs and the surrounding medium is playing a key role in improving the ultrasound signal scattering, resulting in superior imaging contrast and resolution [35,54]. Thus, these results suggest that the MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs demonstrated here have the potential to improve echogenicity in real-time clinical settings.

We have demonstrated the fabrication of MoS2 NFs decorated with CuS NRs and FACDs via Cys—PEI linker for targeted cancer therapy and cancer cell imaging. HR-TEM investigations revealed flower-shaped MoS2 NFs with a size of 350 ± 50 nm, which increased to 500 ± 50 nm after surface decoration. The PCE of MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs was estimated to be 44.2 % under NIR light exposure of 808 nm and 0.5 W.cm−2. The DOX encapsulation was estimated to be 42 %. Though the release was significantly influenced by both pH and NIR light exposure, the release was found to be higher at pH 5.5 in the presence of NIR light. While the MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs showed good biocompatibility against L-929 normal cells up to 100 μg/mL, the anticancer activity of MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs+DOX was found to be 89 % against MDA-MB-231 cells at the same concentration under NIR exposure. The MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs exhibited high anticancer activity under NIR irradiation due to their strong absorbance in the NIR range, targeted cellular accumulation, and thermal damage induced to the cancer cell. The improved ultrasound imaging of MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs is attributed to the high scattering efficiency of CuS NRs and MoS2 NFs. The substantial acoustic impedance mismatch between MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs and the surrounding medium enhances ultrasound signal scattering, resulting in superior imaging contrast and resolution. Hence, the MoS2@CuS/FACDs NFs have a high potential for early cancer cell detection and treatment, live-tracking, and imaging of cancer cells while providing an option for chemo-PTT therapy.

Nishakavya Saravanan: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis. Anandhakumar Sundaramurthy: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Conceptualization. Sukho Park: Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization.
